title: else if Statement
description: Chaining multiple conditions with else if: order matters, and only the first true branch runs.
objectives: Chain multiple conditions using else if
Understand that only the first true branch executes
Understand why the order of conditions matters
Build a simple grade calculator using else if

sections.text: With separate if statements, every single one is checked independently. Here, marks = 85 makes both the second and third conditions true, so this would print Grade BGrade C — not what we want. We need a way to check conditions as one connected chain, where only one branch can win.

else if chains conditions together. C++ checks them in order, top to bottom, and stops at the first one that's true — every condition after that is skipped entirely, even if it would also have been true.

sections.title: Tracing it
points: marks >= 80 is true — print Grade B, then stop checking
marks >= 70 is never even evaluated, because a true branch was already found

sections.text: Written in the wrong order, marks >= 70 is checked first — and since 85 >= 70, that branch wins immediately, printing Grade C for a student who actually deserves Grade B. When conditions overlap like this (a value satisfying more than one), the more specific/restrictive condition needs to come first.

In an if / else if chain, exactly zero or one branch executes — never more than one, no matter how many conditions would technically be true. This is the key difference from writing several separate if statements, where every one is checked independently.

sections.title: Airport security checkpoints
sections.text: Imagine checking travelers against a priority list: VIP first, then Business class, then Economy. Once a traveler is matched to VIP, you don't also check whether they qualify for Economy — the first match wins, and everyone moves on. else if works the same way: the first true condition wins, and the rest are never even checked.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Writing separate if statements instead of else if when only one branch should run — multiple ifs are each checked independently and can all execute.
Ordering broad conditions before narrow ones — a broad marks >= 70 checked before marks >= 90 will always catch high scores too, hiding the more specific branch.
Assuming every condition in the chain gets evaluated — once one is true, the rest are skipped entirely, not just their bodies.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the difference between multiple if statements and an if / else if chain?
questions.answer: Separate if statements are each checked independently and any number of them can run; in an if / else if chain, only the first true condition's branch runs, and the rest are skipped.

questions.question: Why does the order of conditions matter in an else if chain?
questions.answer: Because the first true condition wins and stops the chain — if a broader condition is placed before a more specific one, the specific condition may never be reached even when it would also be true.

questions.question: In a chain of five else if conditions, what's the maximum number of blocks that can execute?
questions.answer: One — an if / else if chain always runs at most a single branch, whichever condition is found true first.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: else if lets multiple conditions be chained together so C++ checks them in order and runs only the first one found true, skipping the rest entirely — unlike separate if statements, which are each checked independently and could all run. Because the first match wins, the order of conditions matters: broader conditions placed before more specific ones can accidentally catch cases meant for a later branch, so conditions are usually ordered from most specific to least specific.

sections.title: Trick to Remember else if
sections.text: else if chain = a priority line. First true condition wins, everyone behind it is skipped. Order from most specific to least specific.
